Concepts.Title: Arrays & Dictionary
Concepts.Description: Working with fixed-size arrays and key-value Dictionary collections.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Arrays

An **array** stores a fixed number of elements of the same type:
```csharp
int[] numbers = new int[] { 10, 20, 30 };
Console.WriteLine(numbers[1]);   // 20
Console.WriteLine(numbers.Length); // 3
```

### Dictionary

A `Dictionary<TKey, TValue>` maps unique keys to values — like a real dictionary maps words to definitions:
```csharp
var capitals = new Dictionary<string, string>();
capitals.Add("France", "Paris");
capitals["Germany"] = "Berlin";     // alternate syntax
Console.WriteLine(capitals["France"]); // Paris
```

Concepts.Title: LINQ: GroupBy, OrderBy & FirstOrDefault
Concepts.Description: Sorting, grouping, and searching collections with advanced LINQ methods.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### More LINQ Power

Beyond `Where` and `Select`, LINQ offers many more operators:

**`OrderBy` / `OrderByDescending`** — sort a collection:
```csharp
var sorted = numbers.OrderBy(n => n);
```

**`GroupBy`** — partition elements into groups sharing a key:
```csharp
var groups = words.GroupBy(w => w[0]); // group by first letter
```

**`FirstOrDefault`** — return the first matching element, or a default if none match:
```csharp
var bob = names.FirstOrDefault(n => n.StartsWith("B"));
// "Bob" or null if not found
```

Concepts.Title: Types & Variables
Concepts.Description: Understanding value types, reference types, and the var keyword.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Types & Variables

C# is a **strongly typed** language. Every variable must have a declared type. There are two fundamental categories:

**Value Types** store data directly: `int`, `double`, `bool`, `char`. When you copy a value type, you get an independent copy.

**Reference Types** store a reference to data on the heap: `string`, `object`, arrays, and classes. Copying a reference type means both variables point to the same object.

You can use the `var` keyword to let the compiler **infer** the type from the right-hand side:
```csharp
var age = 25;       // compiler infers int
var name = "Bob";   // compiler infers string
var pi = 3.14;      // compiler infers double
```

Concepts.Title: Nullable Types & Null Safety
Concepts.Description: Preventing NullReferenceException with nullable types and null-coalescing operators.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Nullable Types & Null Safety

**Nullable value types** let value types hold `null`:
```csharp
int? maybeAge = null;
```

**Null-coalescing operator `??`** provides a fallback when a value is null:
```csharp
string name = null;
Console.WriteLine(name ?? "Unknown"); // Unknown
```

**Null-conditional operator `?.`** short-circuits to null instead of throwing:
```csharp
string upper = name?.ToUpper(); // null (no exception)
```

Combine them for safe chains: `name?.ToUpper() ?? "NONE"`

Concepts.Title: Reflection & Attributes
Concepts.Description: Inspecting type metadata at runtime and building declarative logic with attributes.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Reflection

**Reflection** lets you inspect types, properties, and methods at runtime using the `System.Reflection` namespace:

```csharp
var type = typeof(string);
Console.WriteLine(type.Name); // String

var props = typeof(MyClass).GetProperties();
foreach (var p in props)
    Console.WriteLine(p.Name);
```

### Attributes

**Attributes** attach metadata to code elements. You can then read them via reflection:
```csharp
[Obsolete("Use NewMethod instead")]
public void OldMethod() { }
```

Reflection is the foundation of many frameworks (serializers, ORMs, test runners).

Concepts.Title: Delegates, Action & Func
Concepts.Description: Treating methods as variables using delegates, Action, and Func.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Delegates, Action & Func

In C# you can store a reference to a method in a variable.

**`Func<T, TResult>`** — takes parameters and returns a value:
```csharp
Func<int, int, int> add = (a, b) => a + b;
Console.WriteLine(add(3, 4)); // 7
```

**`Action<T>`** — takes parameters but returns nothing (`void`):
```csharp
Action<string> greet = name => Console.WriteLine($"Hi {name}!");
greet("Alice"); // Hi Alice!
```

Lambda expressions (`=>`) are the most concise way to define these inline.

Concepts.Title: Tuples & Deconstruction
Concepts.Description: Returning multiple values from methods and unpacking them with deconstruction.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Tuples

A **tuple** bundles multiple values into one lightweight object — perfect for returning several values from a method without creating a class:

```csharp
(string Name, int Age) GetPerson() => ("Alice", 30);
var person = GetPerson();
Console.WriteLine(person.Name); // Alice
```

### Deconstruction

You can unpack a tuple directly into separate variables:
```csharp
var (name, age) = GetPerson();
Console.WriteLine($"{name} is {age}"); // Alice is 30
```

Name: System Design
Description: Learn how large-scale systems are built — from DNS to distributed databases to full design interviews.

Concepts.Title: Client-Server Architecture
Concepts.Description: Understanding the request/response model that underpins the web.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Client-Server Architecture

The **client-server model** is the foundation of nearly every networked application. A **client** (browser, mobile app, CLI) sends a **request** to a **server**, which processes it and sends back a **response**.

Key properties:
- The client initiates communication; the server listens and responds.
- **Stateless** servers treat each request independently (e.g., HTTP). No memory of previous requests.
- **Stateful** servers maintain session data between requests (e.g., a WebSocket connection, a database connection).

In system design interviews, always start by identifying **who the clients are** and **what the server needs to do**.

**Example flow:**
```
Browser (client) → HTTP GET /api/users → Web Server → Database → Response (JSON) → Browser
```

Prompts.Description: In the client-server model, which side initiates the request — the client or the server?
A server that treats each request independently with no memory of previous requests is called _______ (one word).
Name two examples of clients in a typical web application (one per line, lowercase).

Concepts.Title: HTTP and REST APIs
Concepts.Description: The protocol and conventions that power web communication.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### HTTP and REST APIs

**HTTP** (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the language clients and servers speak. Every request has a **method**, a **URL**, **headers**, and optionally a **body**.

The four most important HTTP methods:
- **GET** — Retrieve data (read-only, no side effects)
- **POST** — Create a new resource
- **PUT** — Update/replace an existing resource
- **DELETE** — Remove a resource

**Status codes** tell you what happened:
- **2xx** — Success (200 OK, 201 Created)
- **3xx** — Redirect (301 Moved, 304 Not Modified)
- **4xx** — Client error (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found)
- **5xx** — Server error (500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable)

**REST** (Representational State Transfer) is a set of conventions built on HTTP:
- Resources are identified by URLs (e.g., `/api/users/42`)
- Use HTTP methods to express intent (GET to read, POST to create)
- Responses are typically JSON
- The server is **stateless** — no session stored between requests

Prompts.Description: Which HTTP method is used to retrieve data without side effects?

Concepts.Title: DNS and How the Internet Works
Concepts.Description: How domain names are resolved to IP addresses and the path of a web request.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### DNS and How the Internet Works

When you type `www.example.com` into a browser, a chain of steps happens before any HTML appears:

1. **DNS Lookup** — The browser asks a DNS (Domain Name System) server to translate the domain name into an **IP address** (e.g., `93.184.216.34`).
2. **TCP Connection** — The browser opens a TCP connection to the server at that IP address (usually port 80 for HTTP or 443 for HTTPS).
3. **HTTP Request** — The browser sends an HTTP request (e.g., `GET /index.html`).
4. **Server Processing** — The server processes the request, possibly querying a database.
5. **HTTP Response** — The server sends back a response (HTML, JSON, etc.).
6. **Rendering** — The browser renders the response.

**DNS** is often called the "phonebook of the internet." It's a **hierarchical, distributed** system:
- **Root servers** → **TLD servers** (.com, .org) → **Authoritative servers** (the actual domain records)

DNS results are **cached** at multiple levels (browser, OS, ISP) to avoid repeated lookups. The **TTL** (Time To Live) controls how long a cached result is valid.

Concepts.Title: Relational vs Non-Relational Databases
Concepts.Description: SQL vs NoSQL — tables vs documents and when to use each.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Relational vs Non-Relational Databases

**Relational databases (SQL)** — PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server
- Data lives in **tables** with rows and columns
- **Schema** is defined up-front (structured)
- Support **ACID** transactions: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
- Great for: complex queries, joins, data integrity, financial systems

**Non-relational databases (NoSQL)** — MongoDB, DynamoDB, Cassandra, Redis
- Data can be **documents** (JSON), **key-value** pairs, **wide-column**, or **graph**
- Schema is **flexible** (schema-less or schema-on-read)
- Optimized for **horizontal scaling** and high throughput
- Great for: rapid iteration, unstructured data, caching, real-time analytics

**When to choose which:**
- Need complex joins and strict consistency? → **SQL**
- Need flexible schema and massive horizontal scale? → **NoSQL**
- Many real systems use **both** (polyglot persistence)

**ACID** is a key interview term:
- **Atomicity** — Transaction is all-or-nothing
- **Consistency** — Data moves from one valid state to another
- **Isolation** — Concurrent transactions don't interfere
- **Durability** — Committed data survives crashes

Prompts.Description: What does the 'A' in ACID stand for?
List all four ACID properties, one per line, capitalized.

Concepts.Title: Database Indexing
Concepts.Description: How indexes speed up reads and the trade-offs they introduce.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Database Indexing

Without an index, finding a row in a table requires scanning **every row** (a full table scan). An **index** is a separate data structure that lets the database jump directly to matching rows.

Think of it like the index at the back of a textbook — instead of reading every page, you look up the topic and go straight to the page number.

**How it works:**
- Most indexes use a **B-tree** (balanced tree) structure
- The index stores the indexed column's values in sorted order, with pointers to the actual rows
- Lookups go from O(n) to **O(log n)**

**Trade-offs:**
- **Reads get faster** — queries that filter or sort by the indexed column
- **Writes get slower** — every INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE must also update the index
- **Storage increases** — the index itself takes disk space

**Types of indexes:**
- **Primary index** — automatically created on the primary key
- **Secondary index** — manually created on other columns you query frequently
- **Composite index** — covers multiple columns (order matters!)

**Rule of thumb:** Index columns that appear in WHERE clauses, JOIN conditions, and ORDER BY clauses. Don't index everything — each index has a write cost.

Prompts.Description: What data structure do most database indexes use? (hyphenated, lowercase)
Indexes make reads faster but make what operation slower? (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling
Concepts.Description: Two fundamental strategies for handling more load.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling

When your system can't handle the load, you have two options:

**Vertical Scaling (Scale Up)**
- Add more power to a single machine: more CPU, RAM, faster disks
- Simple — no code changes needed
- Has a **hard ceiling** — there's a limit to how big one machine can get
- Creates a **single point of failure**

**Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out)**
- Add more machines and distribute the load across them
- No theoretical ceiling — keep adding servers
- Requires a **load balancer** to distribute traffic
- Code must handle **distributed state** (sessions, caching, database connections)
- More complex but more resilient

**In interviews:**
- Most systems start vertically (simple) and move to horizontal as they grow
- Horizontal scaling is the foundation of every large-scale system design
- Always mention the trade-off: simplicity vs scalability

Prompts.Description: Adding more CPU and RAM to a single machine is called _______ scaling. (one word, lowercase)
Adding more machines to distribute load is called _______ scaling. (one word, lowercase)
What component is required to distribute traffic across multiple servers in a horizontally scaled system? (two words, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Load Balancers
Concepts.Description: Distributing traffic across multiple servers for reliability and performance.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Load Balancers

A **load balancer** sits between clients and a pool of servers, distributing incoming requests so no single server is overwhelmed.

**Common algorithms:**
- **Round Robin** — Requests go to servers in rotation (1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3...)
- **Least Connections** — Send to whichever server has the fewest active connections
- **IP Hash** — Hash the client's IP to always route them to the same server (sticky sessions)
- **Weighted** — Assign more traffic to more powerful servers

**Layer 4 (L4) vs Layer 7 (L7):**
- **L4** — Routes based on IP/port (TCP level). Fast but no visibility into the request content.
- **L7** — Routes based on HTTP content (URL, headers, cookies). Slower but smarter.

**Health checks:** Load balancers periodically ping each server. If a server fails to respond, it's removed from the pool automatically.

**Examples:** AWS ALB/NLB, Nginx, HAProxy, Azure Load Balancer

Prompts.Description: What load balancing algorithm sends requests to servers in simple rotation? (two words, lowercase)
A Layer 7 load balancer can route based on HTTP content. What OSI layer does an L4 load balancer operate at — and what protocol level is that? Answer as: 'layer number - protocol' (lowercase).
How does a load balancer detect that a backend server has gone down? (two words, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Caching Strategies
Concepts.Description: Storing frequently accessed data closer to the consumer for speed.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Caching Strategies

A **cache** stores copies of frequently accessed data in a fast layer (usually in-memory) to avoid expensive recomputation or database queries.

**Cache-aside (Lazy Loading):**
1. App checks the cache first
2. On **cache miss**, app reads from the database
3. App writes the result to the cache for next time
- Most common pattern. Simple but first request is always slow.

**Write-through:**
1. App writes to the cache AND database simultaneously
2. Cache is always up-to-date
- Slower writes, but reads are always fresh

**Write-behind (Write-back):**
1. App writes to cache only
2. Cache asynchronously flushes to database later
- Fast writes, but risk of data loss if cache crashes

**Key concepts:**
- **TTL (Time To Live)** — How long a cached item is valid before it's evicted
- **Cache invalidation** — Deciding when cached data is stale ("the two hardest problems in CS: cache invalidation and naming things")
- **Eviction policies** — LRU (Least Recently Used), LFU (Least Frequently Used)

**Examples:** Redis, Memcached, browser cache, CDN cache

Prompts.Description: What is the caching pattern where the app checks the cache first, then falls back to the database on a miss? (two words, lowercase, hyphenated)
What does TTL stand for in the context of caching? (capitalize each word)
What eviction policy removes the item that hasn't been accessed for the longest time? (abbreviation, uppercase)

Concepts.Title: Content Delivery Networks
Concepts.Description: Serving static content from edge servers close to users worldwide.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Content Delivery Networks (CDNs)

A **CDN** is a geographically distributed network of servers (called **edge servers** or **Points of Presence**) that cache and serve content close to the user.

**How it works:**
1. User in Tokyo requests an image from your US-based server
2. Without CDN: request travels across the Pacific — slow (high latency)
3. With CDN: the image is cached on an edge server in Tokyo — fast

**What CDNs serve:**
- Static assets: images, CSS, JavaScript, videos, fonts
- Some CDNs also support **dynamic content acceleration**

**Benefits:**
- **Reduced latency** — Content served from the nearest edge
- **Lower origin load** — Origin server handles fewer requests
- **DDoS protection** — Distributed network absorbs attack traffic
- **High availability** — If one edge goes down, others take over

**Pull vs Push CDN:**
- **Pull** — Edge fetches content from origin on first request, then caches it
- **Push** — You upload content to the CDN proactively

**Examples:** CloudFlare, AWS CloudFront, Akamai, Azure CDN

Prompts.Description: What does CDN stand for? (capitalize each word)
What are the geographically distributed servers in a CDN commonly called? (two words, lowercase)
In a pull CDN, what triggers the edge server to fetch content from the origin? (three words, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Database Replication
Concepts.Description: Copying data across multiple database servers for reliability and read performance.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Database Replication

**Replication** means maintaining copies of the same data on multiple database servers. It serves two goals: **high availability** (if one server dies, others have the data) and **read scaling** (spread read queries across replicas).

**Leader-Follower (Master-Slave):**
- One **leader** handles all writes
- One or more **followers** replicate the leader's data and handle reads
- Simple and common. If the leader dies, a follower is **promoted**.
- Risk: **replication lag** — followers may serve slightly stale data

**Leader-Leader (Multi-Master):**
- Multiple servers accept writes
- More complex: must handle **write conflicts** (two servers update the same row)
- Used when you need write availability in multiple regions

**Synchronous vs Asynchronous replication:**
- **Synchronous** — Write isn't confirmed until all replicas have it. Strong consistency but slower.
- **Asynchronous** — Write is confirmed as soon as the leader has it. Faster but replicas may lag.

**Replication lag** is a critical concept. If a user writes data and then immediately reads from a follower that hasn't replicated yet, they won't see their own write.

Prompts.Description: In leader-follower replication, which node handles all writes — the leader or the follower?
What is the term for when a follower serves slightly stale data because it hasn't caught up to the leader? (two words, lowercase)
If the leader in a leader-follower setup dies, what happens to a follower? (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: CAP Theorem
Concepts.Description: The fundamental trade-off in distributed systems: pick two of three.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### CAP Theorem

The **CAP theorem** states that a distributed system can provide at most **two of three** guarantees simultaneously:

- **Consistency** — Every read returns the most recent write. All nodes see the same data at the same time.
- **Availability** — Every request receives a response (even if it's not the latest data). No request is denied.
- **Partition Tolerance** — The system continues to operate even when network communication between nodes is lost.

**The reality:** Network partitions **will happen** in any distributed system, so Partition Tolerance is non-negotiable. The real choice is between **Consistency** and **Availability** during a partition:

- **CP systems** (Consistency + Partition Tolerance) — Refuse to serve requests rather than return stale data. Examples: MongoDB (in strict mode), HBase, Redis Cluster.
- **AP systems** (Availability + Partition Tolerance) — Always respond, even if the data might be stale. Examples: Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB.

**Interview tip:** Don't say "pick two." Say "during a network partition, do you prioritize consistency or availability?" That shows deeper understanding.

Prompts.Description: What does the 'C' in CAP stand for?
List all three CAP properties, one per line, capitalized.
A system that always responds to requests even with stale data during a partition is classified as what two-letter CAP combination? (uppercase)

Concepts.Title: Consistency Models
Concepts.Description: Understanding the spectrum from strong consistency to eventual consistency.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Consistency Models

In distributed systems, "consistency" isn't binary — it's a **spectrum**:

**Strong Consistency:**
- After a write completes, every subsequent read (from any node) returns the new value
- Simplest to reason about but **slowest** (requires coordination between nodes)
- Example: A single-leader database with synchronous replication

**Eventual Consistency:**
- After a write, replicas will **eventually** converge to the same value, but reads may return stale data in the meantime
- **Fastest** and most available
- Example: DNS propagation, social media likes count

**Read-Your-Writes Consistency:**
- A user who writes data is guaranteed to see their own write on subsequent reads
- Other users may still see stale data
- Common compromise — implemented with sticky sessions or reading from the leader after a write

**Causal Consistency:**
- If event A caused event B, everyone sees A before B
- Events that are unrelated (concurrent) can be seen in any order

**Interview tip:** When the interviewer says "consistency," ask them **which kind**. It shows you understand the nuances.

Prompts.Description: What consistency model guarantees that every read returns the most recent write? (two words, lowercase)
What consistency model guarantees that a user always sees their own recent writes? (three words, hyphenated, lowercase)
In eventual consistency, replicas will _______ converge to the same value. (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Message Queues
Concepts.Description: Decoupling services with asynchronous message passing.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Message Queues

A **message queue** is a buffer that sits between a **producer** (the service that creates work) and a **consumer** (the service that processes it). This **decouples** the two services so they don't need to be available at the same time.

**Why use queues:**
- **Absorb traffic spikes** — If 10,000 requests arrive at once, the queue holds them and consumers process at their own pace
- **Decouple services** — Producer doesn't need to know about consumer, and vice versa
- **Retry failed work** — If processing fails, the message stays in the queue
- **Scale consumers independently** — Add more consumers when the queue grows

**Delivery guarantees:**
- **At-most-once** — Message may be lost but never delivered twice. Fastest.
- **At-least-once** — Message is never lost but may be delivered twice. Consumer must be **idempotent**.
- **Exactly-once** — Message delivered exactly once. Hardest to achieve (usually done via at-least-once + deduplication).

**Examples:** Apache Kafka (distributed log), RabbitMQ (traditional queue), AWS SQS, Azure Service Bus

**Interview tip:** If a design involves one service triggering work in another, suggest a message queue to decouple them.

Prompts.Description: The service that puts messages into a queue is called a _______. (one word, lowercase)
What delivery guarantee means a message may be delivered more than once but is never lost? (three words, hyphenated, lowercase)
If a consumer might receive the same message twice, it must be designed to handle duplicates safely. What is the term for this property? (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Microservices vs Monoliths
Concepts.Description: Two architectural styles for structuring applications and their trade-offs.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Microservices vs Monoliths

**Monolith:**
- The entire application is a single deployable unit
- All components share the same database and process
- Simple to develop, test, and deploy initially
- Becomes painful at scale: long build times, risky deployments, team bottlenecks

**Microservices:**
- The application is split into small, independent services, each owning its own data
- Services communicate over the network (HTTP, gRPC, message queues)
- Each service can be deployed, scaled, and updated independently
- Different services can use different tech stacks

**Trade-offs of microservices:**
- **Pros:** Independent scaling, independent deployment, fault isolation, team autonomy
- **Cons:** Network latency, distributed debugging, data consistency across services, operational complexity

**When monolith is better:**
- Small team (< 10 engineers)
- Early-stage product where requirements are changing fast
- When you don't yet understand the domain boundaries

**Interview tip:** Don't default to microservices. Start with a monolith, then explain which parts you'd extract as traffic/team grows. This shows pragmatism.

Prompts.Description: An application deployed as a single unit with all components in one process is called a _______. (one word, lowercase)
In a microservices architecture, each service should own its own _______. (one word, lowercase)
For an early-stage product with a small team and shifting requirements, should you start with microservices or a monolith? (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Database Sharding
Concepts.Description: Splitting a database across multiple servers to handle massive data volumes.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Database Sharding

**Sharding** (horizontal partitioning) splits a single database table across multiple servers. Each server (shard) holds a subset of the rows.

**Why shard:**
- A single database server can't hold all the data or handle all the queries
- Replication helps with read scaling, but **writes** still bottleneck on the leader
- Sharding distributes both reads AND writes

**How it works:**
- A **shard key** determines which shard a row belongs to (e.g., `user_id % num_shards`)
- A **routing layer** directs queries to the correct shard

**Challenges:**
- **Hotspots** — If the shard key is poorly chosen, one shard gets all the traffic (e.g., sharding by date means today's shard is always hot)
- **Cross-shard queries** — Queries that span multiple shards are expensive (JOINs become very hard)
- **Resharding** — Adding or removing shards requires redistributing data (use consistent hashing to minimize this)
- **No global transactions** — ACID across shards is extremely difficult

**Interview tip:** Only propose sharding when you've exhausted simpler options (indexing, caching, read replicas). It adds enormous complexity.

Prompts.Description: What determines which shard a row belongs to? (two words, lowercase)
When one shard receives disproportionately more traffic than others due to a bad shard key, this is called a _______. (one word, lowercase)
What hashing technique minimizes data redistribution when adding or removing shards? (two words, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Redundancy and Failover
Concepts.Description: Eliminating single points of failure through duplication and automatic recovery.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Redundancy and Failover

A **single point of failure (SPOF)** is any component whose failure brings down the entire system. The goal of redundancy is to eliminate SPOFs.

**Active-Passive (Hot Standby):**
- One active server handles all traffic
- A passive server sits idle, replicating data
- If the active server fails, the passive server takes over (**failover**)
- Simple but wastes resources (the passive server is idle)

**Active-Active:**
- Multiple servers all handle traffic simultaneously
- If one fails, the others absorb its load
- Better resource utilization but more complex (must handle data synchronization)

**Failover mechanisms:**
- **Health checks** — Monitoring system pings servers; if no response, triggers failover
- **DNS failover** — Update DNS records to point to a healthy server
- **Floating IP** — A virtual IP address that can be reassigned from a failed server to a healthy one

**Where to add redundancy:**
- Web servers (behind a load balancer)
- Databases (replication with automatic failover)
- Load balancers themselves (redundant pair)
- Network paths (multiple ISPs, multiple availability zones)

Prompts.Description: What four-letter abbreviation describes a component whose failure brings down the whole system?
In an active-passive setup, the idle backup server is called the _______ server. (one word, lowercase)
Which redundancy pattern has multiple servers all handling traffic simultaneously? (two words, hyphenated, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Rate Limiting
Concepts.Description: Protecting services from abuse and overload by capping request rates.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Rate Limiting

**Rate limiting** controls how many requests a client can make in a given time window. It protects your system from abuse, brute-force attacks, and accidental overload.

**Common algorithms:**

**Token Bucket:**
- A bucket holds tokens; each request consumes a token
- Tokens are replenished at a fixed rate
- Allows short bursts (bucket can be full) while enforcing an average rate
- Most widely used algorithm

**Sliding Window:**
- Count requests in a rolling time window (e.g., last 60 seconds)
- Smooth enforcement, no burst allowance
- More memory-intensive

**Fixed Window:**
- Count requests in fixed intervals (e.g., per minute)
- Simple but has an edge problem: 100 requests at :59 and 100 at :00 = 200 in 2 seconds

**Where to apply:**
- **API Gateway** — Rate limit per API key or user
- **Per-service** — Protect individual microservices
- **Global** — Limit total throughput to downstream dependencies

**HTTP response:** When rate limited, servers return **429 Too Many Requests**.

Prompts.Description: What HTTP status code indicates a client has been rate limited?
Which rate limiting algorithm uses a bucket of tokens that are replenished at a fixed rate? (two words, lowercase)
The token bucket algorithm allows short _______ of traffic while maintaining an average rate. (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Monitoring and Observability
Concepts.Description: The three pillars of understanding what your system is doing at runtime.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Monitoring and Observability

**Monitoring** tells you when something is wrong. **Observability** tells you **why**.

The **three pillars of observability:**

**1. Metrics:**
- Numeric measurements over time (CPU usage, request latency, error rate, queue depth)
- Aggregated and cheap to store
- Good for dashboards and alerting
- Examples: Prometheus, Datadog, CloudWatch

**2. Logs:**
- Timestamped text records of events ("User 123 logged in", "Error: connection refused")
- Rich detail but expensive at scale
- Must be structured (JSON) and centralized for searching
- Examples: ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana), Splunk

**3. Traces:**
- Follow a single request as it flows through multiple services
- Show latency at each hop — where is time being spent?
- Essential for debugging in microservices
- Examples: Jaeger, Zipkin, AWS X-Ray

**Alerting strategy:**
- Alert on **symptoms** (high error rate, high latency) not causes (CPU at 80%)
- Use severity levels: page (wake someone up) vs warning (check tomorrow)
- Avoid alert fatigue — too many alerts means people ignore them all

Prompts.Description: Name the three pillars of observability, one per line, lowercase.
Which observability pillar follows a single request as it flows through multiple services? (one word, lowercase)
Should you alert on symptoms like high error rate, or on causes like high CPU? (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: SLAs, SLOs, and Error Budgets
Concepts.Description: Defining and measuring reliability targets for your services.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### SLAs, SLOs, and Error Budgets

**SLA (Service Level Agreement):**
- A **contract** between a service provider and a customer
- Defines what happens if reliability targets aren't met (refunds, credits)
- Example: "99.9% uptime or we credit your account"

**SLO (Service Level Objective):**
- An **internal target** that's stricter than the SLA
- The engineering team's goal — if you hit the SLO, you'll comfortably meet the SLA
- Example: "99.95% uptime" (when the SLA promises 99.9%)

**SLI (Service Level Indicator):**
- The **actual measurement** — the metric that tracks whether you're meeting the SLO
- Example: "Percentage of requests completing in under 200ms"

**The "Nines" of availability:**
- 99% = 3.65 days downtime/year
- 99.9% = 8.76 hours downtime/year
- 99.99% = 52.6 minutes downtime/year
- 99.999% = 5.26 minutes downtime/year

**Error Budget:**
- If your SLO is 99.9%, your error budget is 0.1% — that's how much unreliability you're "allowed"
- While you have budget remaining, ship features fast
- When the budget is exhausted, freeze deployments and focus on reliability

Prompts.Description: What does SLA stand for? (capitalize each word)
An SLO of 99.9% uptime means the error budget is what percentage? (answer as a decimal like 0.1)
What is the actual metric that measures whether you're meeting your SLO? (abbreviation, uppercase)

Concepts.Title: Back-of-the-Envelope Estimation
Concepts.Description: Quick math to estimate system capacity — a critical interview skill.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Back-of-the-Envelope Estimation

In system design interviews, you'll be asked to estimate scale. The goal isn't precision — it's showing you can **reason about orders of magnitude**.

**Key numbers to memorize:**
- 1 day = ~86,400 seconds ≈ **100,000 seconds** (round up for easy math)
- 1 million requests/day ≈ **12 requests/second** (1M / 100K)
- 1 KB = 1,000 bytes, 1 MB = 1,000 KB, 1 GB = 1,000 MB, 1 TB = 1,000 GB

**Latency numbers (order of magnitude):**
- L1 cache: ~1 ns
- RAM access: ~100 ns
- SSD read: ~100 μs (microseconds)
- HDD seek: ~10 ms
- Same datacenter round trip: ~0.5 ms
- Cross-country round trip: ~50 ms

**Common estimation framework:**
1. Start with **daily active users (DAU)**
2. Estimate **actions per user per day**
3. Calculate **queries per second (QPS)**: DAU × actions / 86,400
4. Estimate **storage per action** × actions × retention period
5. Estimate **bandwidth**: QPS × average response size

**Example:** 10M DAU, each posts 2 times/day, each post is 1 KB
- QPS: 10M × 2 / 100K = **200 writes/sec**
- Storage/year: 10M × 2 × 1KB × 365 = **~7 TB/year**

Prompts.Description: Approximately how many seconds are in one day? (answer as a number, no commas)
1 million requests per day is approximately how many requests per second? (just the number)
In estimation, after determining DAU and actions per user, you divide by seconds-per-day to get what metric? (abbreviation, uppercase)

Concepts.Title: URL Shortener Design
Concepts.Description: A classic system design interview question: designing a service like bit.ly.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### URL Shortener Design

This is one of the most common system design interview questions. Here's the structured approach:

**Requirements:**
- Given a long URL, generate a short unique URL
- Given a short URL, redirect to the original
- Read-heavy: many more redirects than new URLs created

**Core design:**
1. **Generate a short key** — Take a unique ID and encode it in **base62** (a-z, A-Z, 0-9). A 7-character base62 string gives 62^7 ≈ 3.5 trillion combinations.
2. **Store the mapping** — Key-value store or relational DB: `short_key → long_url`
3. **Redirect** — On GET `/abc1234`, look up the key, return HTTP **301** (permanent redirect) or **302** (temporary redirect)

**Key decisions:**
- **301 vs 302:** 301 is cached by browsers (less server load, but you lose analytics). 302 forces every request through your server (more load, but you can track clicks).
- **ID generation:** Auto-increment ID + base62, or hash the URL (handle collisions)
- **Database:** Read-heavy → use caching (Redis) in front of the database

**Scale considerations:**
- Cache popular URLs (80/20 rule — 20% of URLs get 80% of traffic)
- Shard the database by the first character of the short key
- Use a CDN for geographic distribution

Prompts.Description: What encoding scheme uses a-z, A-Z, and 0-9 to generate short URL keys? (one word, lowercase)
Which HTTP redirect status code is cached by the browser — 301 or 302?
Is a URL shortener service read-heavy or write-heavy? (two words, hyphenated, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Notification System Design
Concepts.Description: Designing a multi-channel notification system for push, email, and SMS.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Notification System Design

A notification system sends messages to users through multiple channels: push notifications, email, SMS, and in-app.

**High-level architecture:**
1. **Trigger service** — Something happens (new message, order shipped) that generates a notification event
2. **Message queue** — Events are placed on a queue to decouple triggering from delivery
3. **Notification service** — Reads from the queue and routes to the appropriate channel
4. **Channel adapters** — Separate workers for each channel (push, email, SMS) with their own retry logic

**Key design decisions:**

**Fan-out:** One event may need to reach millions of users ("new feature announcement"). Use a message queue with multiple consumers.

**Delivery guarantees:** Notifications should be **at-least-once**. Duplicate notifications are annoying but acceptable; lost notifications are not. Use idempotency keys to reduce duplicates.

**User preferences:** Users should control what channels they receive notifications on. Store preferences per user, check before sending.

**Rate limiting:** Don't spam users. Limit notifications per user per time window.

**Retry with backoff:** If a channel fails (email server down), retry with **exponential backoff** — wait 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, etc.

Prompts.Description: What component decouples the notification trigger from the delivery? (two words, lowercase)
What retry strategy doubles the wait time between each attempt? (two words, lowercase)
What delivery guarantee is preferred for notifications — at-most-once or at-least-once? (hyphenated, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Chat System Design
Concepts.Description: Real-time messaging architecture using WebSockets and presence tracking.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Chat System Design

Chat is one of the most asked system design topics because it touches real-time communication, storage, and presence.

**Core challenge:** HTTP is request-response. Chat needs **bidirectional, real-time** communication.

**WebSockets:**
- A persistent, full-duplex connection between client and server
- Both sides can send messages at any time (unlike HTTP where only the client initiates)
- The client opens a WebSocket connection on login and keeps it alive

**Architecture:**
1. **Connection service** — Manages WebSocket connections. Maps `user_id → server_id` so you know which server a user is connected to.
2. **Message service** — Stores messages in a database. Writes are done asynchronously.
3. **Presence service** — Tracks who's online. Uses heartbeats — if no heartbeat for N seconds, mark offline.

**Message delivery flow:**
1. User A sends a message via WebSocket to their connected server
2. Server looks up which server User B is connected to
3. Message is forwarded to User B's server, which pushes it to User B's WebSocket
4. If User B is offline, message is stored and delivered when they reconnect

**Storage:**
- Recent messages: fast key-value store (e.g., Redis)
- Historical messages: append-optimized database (e.g., Cassandra, partitioned by conversation_id)

**Group chat:** Fan-out the message to all group members. For large groups, use a message queue.

Prompts.Description: What protocol provides persistent, bidirectional communication between client and server? (one word, capitalized)
How does a presence service detect that a user has gone offline? (one word, lowercase)
If User B is offline when a message arrives, what must the system do? (answer: store and deliver later, in exactly those words)

Concepts.Title: News Feed Design
Concepts.Description: Designing a social media timeline with fan-out strategies.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### News Feed Design

A news feed (Twitter timeline, Facebook feed) shows a personalized stream of posts from people a user follows.

**The core problem:** When User A has 1,000 followers and posts something, how do those 1,000 followers see it in their feed?

**Fan-out on Write (Push model):**
- When a user posts, immediately write that post to the feed of every follower
- **Pros:** Reading the feed is instant — it's pre-computed
- **Cons:** Writing is expensive for users with millions of followers (celebrity problem)
- Good for users with small follower counts

**Fan-out on Read (Pull model):**
- When a user opens their feed, query all the people they follow and merge their recent posts
- **Pros:** Writing a post is instant — just one write
- **Cons:** Reading is slow — must query and merge many sources
- Good for users with lots of followings who are rarely active

**Hybrid approach (what most systems use):**
- For normal users: fan-out on write (pre-compute feeds)
- For celebrities (millions of followers): fan-out on read (compute on demand when followers request)
- This avoids the celebrity problem while keeping reads fast for most users

**Ranking:** Feeds aren't just chronological. A ranking algorithm scores posts by relevance, recency, engagement, and relationship strength.

**Caching:** Cache the top N items of each user's feed in Redis. Most users only look at the first page.

Prompts.Description: The strategy that pre-computes feeds by writing to all followers at post time is called fan-out on _______. (one word, lowercase)
A user with millions of followers makes fan-out on write expensive. What is this problem commonly called? (two words, lowercase)
Most production feed systems use a _______ approach: fan-out on write for normal users, fan-out on read for celebrities. (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Designing for Scale: Putting It All Together
Concepts.Description: A structured framework for tackling any system design interview question.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Designing for Scale: Putting It All Together

Use this **four-step framework** in every system design interview:

**Step 1: Requirements Clarification (3-5 minutes)**
- What are the core features? (Don't design everything — focus on what's asked)
- Who are the users? How many?
- What are the read/write ratios?
- What consistency and latency requirements exist?
- Ask about scale: DAU, QPS, storage

**Step 2: High-Level Design (10-15 minutes)**
- Draw the main components: clients, load balancer, web servers, database, cache
- Show the data flow for the primary use cases
- Identify the API endpoints (e.g., POST /messages, GET /feed)
- Choose the database type (SQL vs NoSQL) and justify it

**Step 3: Deep Dive (10-15 minutes)**
- The interviewer will pick 1-2 components to zoom into
- This is where you use the concepts from this curriculum:
  - Caching strategy, database sharding, message queues, replication
  - Back-of-the-envelope calculations
  - Trade-offs (consistency vs availability, push vs pull)

**Step 4: Wrap-Up (3-5 minutes)**
- Summarize the design
- Discuss bottlenecks and how you'd monitor them
- Mention what you'd improve with more time (rate limiting, analytics, A/B testing)

**Key interviewer signals:**
- **Good:** You discuss trade-offs, ask clarifying questions, acknowledge limitations
- **Bad:** You jump to a solution without understanding requirements, or design for Google scale when the problem is a startup

Prompts.Description: What is the first step in a system design interview? (two words, lowercase)
List the four steps of the design interview framework, one per line, lowercase. (requirements clarification, high-level design, deep dive, wrap-up)
Prompts.ExpectedOutput:
requirements clarification
high-level design
deep dive
wrap-up

Prompts.Description: In a system design interview, interviewers value seeing you discuss the pros and cons of different approaches. What is the single word for this? (lowercase, hyphenated)

Concepts.Title: Cache-Aside & Read-Through Patterns
Concepts.Description: The two dominant read-caching strategies and their trade-offs.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Cache-Aside & Read-Through Patterns

These are the two most common **read-path** caching strategies.

**Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading):**
1. Application checks the cache
2. On a **miss**, the **application** fetches from the database
3. Application writes the result into the cache
4. Subsequent reads are served from cache

- **Pro:** Only caches data that's actually requested
- **Con:** First request for any key is always slow (cold start)
- **Con:** If many requests hit the same expired key at once, they all miss and slam the database — this is called the **thundering herd** problem

Mitigations for thundering herd: **lock/mutex** on cache population, **stale-while-revalidate** (serve stale data while refreshing in the background).

**Read-Through:**
1. Application always reads from the cache
2. On a miss, the **cache itself** fetches from the database (via a configured loader)
3. Cache stores the result and returns it

- **Pro:** Application code is simpler — it only talks to the cache
- **Con:** Cache must know how to talk to your data store (coupling)
- **Con:** Still has cold-start issues

**When to use which:**
- **Cache-aside** when you want full control and your data access patterns are complex
- **Read-through** when you want a cleaner abstraction and your cache library supports loaders (e.g., Caffeine, Guava)

Prompts.Description: In cache-aside, who fetches from the database on a cache miss — the application or the cache? (one word, lowercase)
What problem occurs when many requests hit an expired cache key simultaneously? (two words, lowercase)
In read-through caching, who is responsible for loading data on a cache miss — the application or the cache? (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Write-Through & Write-Behind Caching
Concepts.Description: Write-side caching patterns and the consistency vs. latency spectrum.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Write-Through & Write-Behind Caching

Once you understand read-path caching (cache-aside, read-through), you need to decide what happens on **writes**.

**Write-Through:**
1. Application writes to the cache
2. Cache **synchronously** writes to the database before confirming
- **Pro:** Cache and database are always consistent
- **Con:** Every write pays the latency of both the cache and the database
- Best for: systems where **read consistency is critical** (e.g., user profiles, account balances)

**Write-Behind (Write-Back):**
1. Application writes to the cache only
2. Cache **asynchronously** flushes to the database later (batched or on a timer)
- **Pro:** Extremely fast writes — application only waits for the cache
- **Con:** Risk of **data loss** if the cache crashes before flushing
- **Con:** Database may serve stale data if read directly
- Best for: high-throughput systems where some data loss is acceptable (e.g., analytics counters, session data)

**Write-Around:**
1. Application writes **directly to the database**, skipping the cache
2. Cache is only populated on subsequent reads (via cache-aside or read-through)
- **Pro:** Avoids polluting the cache with data that may never be read
- **Con:** First read after a write is always a cache miss
- Best for: write-heavy workloads where most written data is rarely re-read

**Comparison Summary:**
| Pattern | Write Latency | Consistency | Data Loss Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Write-Through | High | Strong | None |
| Write-Behind | Low | Eventual | Yes |
| Write-Around | Medium | Eventual | None |

Prompts.Description: In write-through caching, are writes to the cache and database done synchronously or asynchronously? (one word, lowercase)
What is the main risk of write-behind caching? (two words, lowercase)
Which write caching pattern skips the cache entirely on writes and only populates it on reads? (one hyphenated word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Distributed Caching
Concepts.Description: Scaling caches across nodes with Redis, Memcached, and consistent hashing.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Distributed Caching

A single cache node has limited memory and is a single point of failure. **Distributed caching** spreads data across multiple nodes.

**Consistent Hashing:**
- Maps both keys and nodes onto a virtual ring
- A key is assigned to the first node clockwise on the ring
- When a node is added or removed, only a fraction of keys need to move (not all of them)
- Without consistent hashing, adding a node to a naive hash(key) % N scheme invalidates nearly every key

**Redis:**
- In-memory data structure store (strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets)
- Supports **replication** (master-replica) for read scaling and failover
- **Redis Cluster** partitions data across multiple masters using hash slots (16,384 slots)
- Persistence: **RDB** (periodic snapshots) and **AOF** (append-only log of every write)
- Supports pub/sub, Lua scripting, transactions

**Memcached:**
- Simpler, pure key-value cache
- Multi-threaded (better CPU utilization per node)
- No persistence, no replication built-in
- Best for simple caching where Redis features aren't needed

**Redis vs Memcached:**
| Feature | Redis | Memcached |
|---|---|---|
| Data structures | Rich | Key-value only |
| Persistence | RDB + AOF | None |
| Replication | Built-in | External |
| Threading | Single-threaded (multi in 6.0+) | Multi-threaded |

Prompts.Description: Name the most popular open-source in-memory data store used for distributed caching. (one word, lowercase)
What hashing technique minimizes key redistribution when a cache node is added or removed? (two words, lowercase)
What are the two Redis persistence mechanisms? (abbreviations, slash-separated, uppercase)

Concepts.Title: Circuit Breaker Pattern
Concepts.Description: Preventing cascading failures in distributed systems with fail-fast behavior.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Circuit Breaker Pattern

In a microservices architecture, one slow or failing service can cascade failures to every service that depends on it. The **circuit breaker** pattern prevents this by failing fast.

**Three States:**

1. **Closed** (normal operation)
   - Requests flow through to the downstream service
   - Failures are counted
   - If failures exceed a **threshold** (e.g., 5 failures in 10 seconds), the breaker trips to Open

2. **Open** (fail-fast)
   - All requests are **immediately rejected** without calling the downstream service
   - Returns a fallback response or error
   - After a **timeout period**, transitions to Half-Open

3. **Half-Open** (testing recovery)
   - Allows a **limited number of test requests** through
   - If they succeed, the breaker resets to Closed
   - If they fail, the breaker goes back to Open

**Why it matters:**
- Without a circuit breaker, a failing service consumes threads and connections in every caller, creating a **cascading failure**
- With a circuit breaker, the system **degrades gracefully** — callers get fast errors instead of hanging

**Libraries:**
- **.NET:** Polly
- **Java:** Resilience4j (successor to Hystrix)
- **Go:** gobreaker

**Related patterns:** Retry with exponential backoff, bulkhead isolation, timeout pattern

Prompts.Description: What are the three states of a circuit breaker? (comma-separated, lowercase)
When a circuit breaker is in the open state, are requests sent to the downstream service? (yes or no, lowercase)
In which circuit breaker state does it allow a limited number of test requests through to check if the service has recovered? (one hyphenated word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: API Gateway & Reverse Proxy
Concepts.Description: A single entry point for clients that handles routing, auth, and rate limiting.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### API Gateway & Reverse Proxy

**Reverse Proxy:**
A server that sits **in front of backend servers** and forwards client requests to them. Clients never talk to backends directly.

Responsibilities:
- **Load balancing** — distribute requests across servers
- **SSL termination** — handle HTTPS so backends don't have to
- **Compression** and static file serving
- **Examples:** Nginx, HAProxy, Envoy

**API Gateway:**
A specialized reverse proxy designed for **microservices architectures**. It adds higher-level features on top of basic proxying.

Additional responsibilities:
- **Request routing** — route `/api/users` to the Users service, `/api/orders` to the Orders service
- **Authentication & authorization** — validate tokens before requests reach services
- **Rate limiting** — throttle abusive clients
- **Request/response transformation** — aggregate multiple service calls into one client response
- **Examples:** Kong, AWS API Gateway, Azure API Management

**BFF (Backend For Frontend):**
A pattern where you create a **dedicated API gateway per client type** (web, mobile, IoT). Each BFF tailors the API surface to its client's needs instead of one gateway trying to serve everyone.

**API Gateway vs Load Balancer:**
- A load balancer distributes traffic to instances of the **same service**
- An API gateway routes traffic to **different services** based on the request path/headers
- In practice, they are often used together

Prompts.Description: What type of proxy sits in front of backend servers and forwards client requests to them? (two words, lowercase)
What does BFF stand for in the API gateway context? (capitalize each word)
Name two common responsibilities of an API gateway besides routing. (comma-separated, lowercase)

Name: Agentic AI Architecture
Description: Design, build, and reason about AI agent systems — from LLM fundamentals to multi-agent orchestration and production safety.

Concepts.Title: How LLMs Work: Tokens, Probabilities & Context Windows
Concepts.Description: The mechanics behind large language models — tokenization, next-token prediction, and context limits.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### How LLMs Work

**Large Language Models (LLMs)** like GPT-4, Claude, and Llama are neural networks trained to predict the **next token** in a sequence. Understanding their mechanics is essential for designing agent systems.

**Tokens:**
- LLMs don't read characters or words — they read **tokens**, which are sub-word chunks
- "unhappiness" → ["un", "happiness"] (2 tokens)
- A rough rule: 1 token ≈ 4 characters in English, or ~¾ of a word
- Every API call is billed by tokens (input + output)

**Next-Token Prediction:**
- The model takes all input tokens and produces a **probability distribution** over possible next tokens
- It samples from this distribution to pick the next token, then repeats
- This is why LLMs are "auto-regressive" — each token depends on all previous tokens

**Context Window:**
- The **context window** is the maximum number of tokens the model can see at once (input + output combined)
- GPT-4o: ~128K tokens. Claude: ~200K tokens. Smaller models: 4K–32K
- Once you exceed the window, the model literally cannot see earlier tokens
- Larger windows cost more and increase latency

**Why This Matters for Agents:**
- Every tool call result, memory retrieval, and conversation turn consumes tokens
- An agent that stuffs its entire history into every call will hit context limits and get expensive fast
- Designing what goes into the context window is one of the most important architectural decisions

Prompts.Description: LLMs predict the next _____ in a sequence. (one word, lowercase)
What is the term for the maximum number of tokens an LLM can process in a single call? (two words, lowercase)
An LLM generates text by producing a probability distribution over possible next tokens and then _____ from it. (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Embeddings & Semantic Similarity
Concepts.Description: How text is converted to numerical vectors and used for meaning-based comparison.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Embeddings & Semantic Similarity

**Embeddings** are numerical representations of text (vectors of floating-point numbers, typically 256–3072 dimensions) that capture **meaning**. Texts with similar meanings have vectors that are close together in this high-dimensional space.

**How They Work:**
- An embedding model (like OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small) converts text into a fixed-size vector
- "How do I reset my password?" and "I forgot my login credentials" produce vectors that are **close** to each other
- "How do I reset my password?" and "What's the weather today?" produce vectors that are **far apart**

**Cosine Similarity:**
- The standard way to measure closeness between two vectors
- Ranges from -1 (opposite) to 1 (identical meaning)
- Typically, similarity > 0.8 indicates strong semantic match

**Why This Matters for Agents:**
- **RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation):** Embeddings power the "retrieval" step — you embed a user's question, then find the most similar document chunks
- **Semantic routing:** An orchestrator can embed a user request and route it to the right specialized agent based on similarity
- **Deduplication:** Detect when two questions mean the same thing even if worded differently
- **Memory search:** An agent can search its past interactions by meaning, not just keyword match

**Key Trade-offs:**
- Embedding models are separate from chat models — you need to call them explicitly
- Higher-dimensional embeddings capture more nuance but cost more to store and search
- Embeddings are language/domain sensitive — an embedding model trained on English legal text may not work well for Japanese cooking recipes

Prompts.Description: What is the standard metric for measuring closeness between two embedding vectors? (two words, lowercase)
Embeddings convert text into fixed-size vectors of _____ numbers. (one hyphenated term, lowercase)
In RAG, embeddings power which step — retrieval or generation? (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Temperature, Top-p & Sampling Strategies
Concepts.Description: Controlling the randomness and creativity of LLM outputs.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Temperature, Top-p & Sampling Strategies

When an LLM produces a probability distribution over next tokens, **sampling parameters** control how the final token is chosen. These settings dramatically affect output quality for different use cases.

**Temperature:**
- Controls the "sharpness" of the probability distribution
- **Temperature = 0:** Always picks the highest-probability token (deterministic, "greedy")
- **Temperature = 0.7:** Moderate randomness — good for creative but coherent text
- **Temperature = 1.5:** Very random — creative but may produce nonsense
- Think of it as a dial between "predictable" and "creative"

**Top-p (Nucleus Sampling):**
- Instead of considering all tokens, only consider the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds **p**
- **Top-p = 0.1:** Only consider the top ~10% most likely tokens (very focused)
- **Top-p = 0.9:** Consider the top ~90% (more diverse)
- Dynamically adjusts the candidate pool based on the distribution shape

**Practical Guidelines for Agents:**
- **Code generation / structured output:** Temperature 0, or very low (0–0.2). You want deterministic, correct answers.
- **Creative writing / brainstorming:** Temperature 0.7–1.0
- **Tool-calling agents:** Temperature 0. Wrong function arguments are worse than boring text.
- **Don't set both temperature and top-p aggressively** — pick one to tune, leave the other at default

**Why This Matters:**
- An agent that calls tools with temperature=1 will hallucinate function arguments
- A coding agent with temperature=0 produces consistent, reproducible output
- Understanding these parameters lets you diagnose "flaky" agent behavior — often it's just sampling randomness

Prompts.Description: What temperature value makes LLM output fully deterministic? (single digit)
What sampling strategy considers only tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds a threshold? (two words, with hyphen, lowercase)
For a tool-calling agent, should temperature be high or low? (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Prompt Engineering Fundamentals
Concepts.Description: Crafting effective prompts — the system prompt, few-shot examples, and instruction clarity.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Prompt Engineering Fundamentals

A **prompt** is the instruction you give an LLM. The quality of the output is directly proportional to the quality of the prompt. For agent systems, prompt engineering is infrastructure — it determines how reliably your agent behaves.

**The Three Prompt Roles (Chat API):**
- **System:** Sets the agent's identity, rules, and constraints. Persists across the conversation.
- **User:** The human's input or the orchestrator's instruction to a sub-agent.
- **Assistant:** The model's previous responses (used for multi-turn context).

**Key Techniques:**
1. **Be specific, not vague:** "Summarize this in 3 bullet points" beats "Summarize this"
2. **Few-shot examples:** Show the model 2–3 input/output examples of exactly what you want
3. **Chain-of-thought (CoT):** Add "Think step by step" or "Explain your reasoning before answering" to improve accuracy on complex tasks
4. **Negative constraints:** "Do NOT include code examples" is often more effective than only saying what to include
5. **Output format specification:** "Respond in JSON with keys: name, age, city" dramatically improves parseability

**System Prompt Best Practices:**
- Define the agent's role clearly: "You are a senior code reviewer..."
- List what the agent CAN and CANNOT do
- Specify the output format explicitly
- Include edge case instructions: "If the user asks something outside your scope, say so"

**Why This Matters for Agents:**
- A poorly prompted agent will hallucinate tools, ignore constraints, and produce unparseable output
- The system prompt is effectively your agent's "source code" — treat it with the same rigor
- Changing a single word in a system prompt can flip an agent from reliable to broken

Prompts.Description: In the Chat API, which message role sets the agent's identity and rules? (one word, lowercase)
What technique involves showing the model 2-3 input/output examples? (two words, hyphenated, lowercase)
Adding 'Think step by step' to a prompt is an example of which reasoning technique? (abbreviation, three letters, uppercase)

Concepts.Title: Structured Output & Output Parsing
Concepts.Description: Getting reliable, machine-readable output from LLMs — JSON mode, schemas, and parsing strategies.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Structured Output & Output Parsing

LLMs produce free-form text by default. For agent systems, you need **structured, parseable output** — JSON, function calls, or specific formats that downstream code can consume reliably.

**The Problem:**
- Ask an LLM "List three cities" and you might get: a numbered list, a comma-separated string, a paragraph, or JSON — depending on its mood
- An agent that produces unparseable output breaks the entire pipeline

**Solutions (from most to least reliable):**

1. **Function Calling / Tool Use (best):**
   - The model outputs a structured function call with typed arguments
   - The API guarantees valid JSON matching your schema
   - Supported by OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and most providers
   - This is how agents invoke tools — the model "calls" a function, your code executes it

2. **JSON Mode:**
   - Forces the model to output valid JSON (but doesn't guarantee a specific schema)
   - You still need to validate the shape of the JSON yourself

3. **Schema Enforcement (Pydantic, Zod, etc.):**
   - Libraries like Instructor (Python) or Zod (TypeScript) define a schema and retry/fix if the output doesn't match
   - Adds a validation layer on top of raw LLM output

4. **Prompt-based (least reliable):**
   - "Respond in JSON with keys: name, age" — works most of the time, fails unpredictably
   - Never use this as your only strategy in production

**Why This Matters:**
- Agents chain multiple LLM calls — if step 2 can't parse step 1's output, the whole chain fails
- Function calling is the backbone of tool-using agents
- The reliability of your agent is bounded by the reliability of its output parsing

Prompts.Description: What is the most reliable way for an LLM to produce structured output for agents? (two words, lowercase)
JSON Mode guarantees valid JSON but does NOT guarantee a specific _____. (one word, lowercase)
Name a Python library that enforces output schemas on LLM responses by defining models and retrying on mismatch. (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: What Is an AI Agent? Autonomy vs Automation
Concepts.Description: Defining agents, distinguishing them from simple LLM calls, and understanding the autonomy spectrum.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### What Is an AI Agent?

An **AI agent** is a system where an LLM **decides what to do next** in a loop — it observes, reasons, acts, and repeats until a goal is met. This is fundamentally different from a single LLM call.

**The Spectrum:**
1. **Simple LLM Call:** User sends prompt → model responds → done. No decisions, no tools.
2. **Chain:** A fixed sequence of LLM calls (summarize → translate → format). Predictable, no branching.
3. **Router:** LLM picks which of N paths to take based on input, but each path is fixed.
4. **Agent:** LLM decides which tools to call, in what order, and whether to keep going. The control flow is determined at runtime by the model.

**Core Agent Loop:**
```
while not done:
    observation = get_current_state()
    thought = llm.reason(observation)
    action = llm.decide_action(thought)
    result = execute(action)
    done = llm.evaluate(result)
```

**Key Properties of Agents:**
- **Goal-directed:** They work toward an objective, not just respond to a prompt
- **Tool-using:** They can call external functions (search, databases, APIs, code execution)
- **Iterative:** They run in a loop, refining their approach based on results
- **Non-deterministic:** The same input may produce different action sequences

**When to Use Agents vs. Simpler Patterns:**
- Use a **simple call** when the task is straightforward (summarize, translate, classify)
- Use a **chain** when you know the exact steps ahead of time
- Use a **router** when you need to pick one of several known workflows
- Use an **agent** when the task requires dynamic decision-making, tool use, or multi-step reasoning where the path isn't known in advance

**The Over-Engineering Trap:**
Not everything needs an agent. If you can solve the problem with a chain or a router, do that — agents are harder to debug, more expensive, and less predictable.

Prompts.Description: In the agent spectrum, what is a fixed sequence of LLM calls with no branching called? (one word, lowercase)
What is the key difference between a router and an agent? An agent's control flow is determined at _____. (one word, lowercase)
Name the three core properties of an agent. (comma-separated, lowercase, alphabetical order)

Concepts.Title: Tool Use & Function Calling
Concepts.Description: How agents interact with external systems through function definitions and tool invocations.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Tool Use & Function Calling

**Tool use** is what transforms an LLM from a text generator into an agent that can *do things*. The model doesn't execute tools itself — it outputs a structured request that your code fulfills.

**How It Works:**
1. You define available tools with names, descriptions, and parameter schemas
2. You send the user's message plus the tool definitions to the LLM
3. The LLM decides whether to respond directly OR call one or more tools
4. If it calls a tool, your code executes the function and sends the result back
5. The LLM incorporates the result and either responds or calls more tools

**Tool Definition Example (conceptual):**
```
Tool: search_database
Description: Search the product database by query string
Parameters:
  - query (string, required): The search term
  - limit (integer, optional): Max results, default 10
```

**Design Principles:**
- **Clear descriptions matter more than clever names.** The model reads the description to decide when to use a tool.
- **Fewer tools = more reliable.** An agent with 50 tools will misroute. Start with 3-5.
- **Constrain parameters.** Use enums and required fields. Don't give the model a freeform string when a dropdown will do.
- **Return useful errors.** When a tool fails, return a descriptive error — the agent can often self-correct.

**Parallel Tool Calls:**
- Modern APIs support the model calling multiple tools simultaneously
- Example: An agent asked "What's the weather in NYC and London?" can call `get_weather("NYC")` and `get_weather("London")` in parallel
- Your orchestration code must handle multiple results coming back

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Tool descriptions that are ambiguous → model picks the wrong tool
- Missing parameter validation → garbage in, crash out
- Not returning tool results to the model → it hallucinates the outcome

Prompts.Description: Does the LLM execute tools itself, or does your code execute them? (two words, lowercase)
What matters more for reliable tool selection — the tool's name or its description? (one word, lowercase)
When a tool call fails, what should you return to the model instead of crashing? (two words, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: ReAct: Reasoning + Acting
Concepts.Description: The foundational agent pattern that interleaves thinking with tool use.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### ReAct: Reasoning + Acting

**ReAct** (Reason + Act) is the most influential agent pattern. It interleaves **reasoning** (thinking about what to do) with **acting** (calling tools or taking actions), creating a visible chain of thought-action-observation.

**The ReAct Loop:**
```
Thought: I need to find the user's order status. Let me search the database.
Action: search_orders(user_id="12345")
Observation: [Order #789: shipped, tracking ABC123]
Thought: I found the order. It's shipped. Let me provide the tracking number.
Action: respond("Your order #789 has shipped! Tracking: ABC123")
```

**Why ReAct Works:**
- **Explicit reasoning** before each action reduces errors — the model "thinks before it acts"
- **Observations ground the model** in real data, reducing hallucination
- The thought-action-observation trace is **debuggable** — you can see exactly why the agent did what it did

**ReAct vs. Chain-of-Thought (CoT):**
- **CoT:** Model reasons through a problem internally, then gives a final answer. No tool use.
- **ReAct:** Model reasons AND acts, incorporating real-world data into its reasoning.
- ReAct is more powerful for tasks that require external information.

**Implementation:**
- Most agent frameworks (LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen) use ReAct as their default pattern
- The model's system prompt instructs it to think before acting
- Your code parses the model's output to extract actions, executes them, and feeds results back

**When ReAct Struggles:**
- Very long tasks (10+ steps) — the reasoning chain gets noisy and the model loses track
- Tasks requiring planning ahead — ReAct is greedy (one step at a time), not strategic
- High-speed requirements — each thought-action cycle is a full LLM call

Prompts.Description: ReAct stands for Reasoning plus _____. (one word, capitalize first letter)
What are the three phases that repeat in a ReAct loop? (comma-separated, lowercase)
ReAct is described as greedy because it reasons one step at a time rather than doing what? (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Planning & Reflection Loops
Concepts.Description: Moving beyond greedy step-by-step reasoning to plan-then-execute and self-correcting architectures.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Planning & Reflection Loops

ReAct is powerful but greedy — it decides one action at a time. For complex tasks, agents benefit from **planning ahead** and **reflecting on their progress**.

**Plan-then-Execute:**
1. The agent first creates a high-level plan (a list of steps)
2. It executes each step, checking off progress
3. If a step fails, it can revise the plan without starting over

Example:
```
Plan:
1. Search for the user's account
2. Check their subscription tier
3. Look up available upgrade options
4. Present recommendations

Executing step 1... ✓
Executing step 2... ✓
Executing step 3... failed (API timeout)
Revising plan: retry step 3 with fallback data source
```

**Reflection:**
- After completing a task (or failing), the agent evaluates its own performance
- "Did I answer the user's actual question? Did I miss anything? Was my approach efficient?"
- This self-assessment can be a separate LLM call that critiques the agent's output

**Inner Critic Pattern:**
- Agent A produces a response
- Agent B (or the same model with a different prompt) critiques it
- Agent A revises based on the critique
- Repeat until the critic approves or a max iteration count is hit

**Practical Considerations:**
- Planning adds latency (extra LLM call up front) but improves success on multi-step tasks
- Reflection loops can get stuck — always set a maximum number of iterations
- The quality of reflection depends heavily on the prompt: "What did you miss?" works better than "Is this good?"
- Not every task needs planning. Simple queries should skip straight to execution.

**When to Use What:**
- **ReAct:** 1–5 step tasks, straightforward tool use
- **Plan-then-Execute:** 5–15 step tasks, tasks with dependencies between steps
- **Reflection:** Tasks where correctness matters more than speed (code review, analysis)

Prompts.Description: In the plan-then-execute pattern, what does the agent create before taking any actions? (one word, lowercase)
In the Inner Critic pattern, one agent produces a response and another agent does what to it? (one word, lowercase)
What must you always set on a reflection loop to prevent it from running forever? (two words, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Agentic Design Patterns Overview
Concepts.Description: A taxonomy of common agent architectures and when to apply each one.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Agentic Design Patterns Overview

Now that you know the building blocks (tool use, ReAct, planning, reflection), let's map out the **landscape of agent architectures** so you can pick the right one for a given problem.

**Pattern 1: Single Agent + Tools**
- One LLM with access to tools in a ReAct loop
- Best for: focused tasks with a clear goal (customer support, data lookup)
- Weakness: struggles with tasks requiring multiple areas of expertise

**Pattern 2: Router / Dispatcher**
- A lightweight LLM classifies the request and routes it to a specialized handler
- Each handler can be a simple chain, another agent, or a deterministic function
- Best for: multi-domain systems ("Is this a billing question or a technical question?")
- Weakness: routing errors cascade; the router must be highly reliable

**Pattern 3: Pipeline / DAG**
- Tasks flow through a directed graph of processing steps
- Some steps are LLM calls, some are deterministic code
- Best for: well-defined workflows (document processing, data enrichment)
- Weakness: inflexible to unexpected inputs

**Pattern 4: Orchestrator-Worker**
- A "manager" agent breaks down the task and delegates subtasks to specialized "worker" agents
- The orchestrator collects results and synthesizes a final answer
- Best for: complex research, multi-step analysis, code generation across files
- Weakness: orchestrator can lose track of the big picture; expensive (many LLM calls)

**Pattern 5: Debate / Ensemble**
- Multiple agents independently attempt the same task
- A judge agent (or voting mechanism) picks the best result
- Best for: high-stakes decisions where accuracy matters more than cost
- Weakness: 3-5x the cost of a single agent

**Choosing the Right Pattern:**
Ask: How complex is the task? How many domains does it span? How important is accuracy vs. speed vs. cost?
- Simple + single domain → Single Agent
- Simple + multiple domains → Router
- Complex + well-defined steps → Pipeline
- Complex + dynamic steps → Orchestrator-Worker
- Critical + accuracy paramount → Debate/Ensemble

Prompts.Description: Which pattern has a manager agent that breaks down tasks and delegates to specialized workers? (one word, lowercase)
Which agent pattern is best for high-stakes decisions where accuracy matters more than cost? (one word, lowercase)
For a simple task spanning multiple domains, which pattern should you pick? (one word, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
Concepts.Description: Grounding LLM responses in real data by retrieving relevant documents before generating.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

**RAG** is the most important pattern for making LLMs useful with your own data. Instead of fine-tuning the model or stuffing everything into the prompt, you **retrieve** relevant information at query time and include it in the context.

**The RAG Pipeline:**
1. **Index (offline):** Split your documents into chunks → embed each chunk → store in a vector database
2. **Query (online):** Embed the user's question → search the vector DB for similar chunks → return top-K results
3. **Generate:** Pass the retrieved chunks + user question to the LLM → model generates an answer grounded in the data

**Why RAG Over Fine-Tuning?**
- **Up-to-date:** RAG uses current data; fine-tuning bakes in training data that gets stale
- **Transparent:** You can see which documents the answer came from (citations)
- **Cheaper:** No expensive training runs; just update your document index
- **Auditable:** If the answer is wrong, check the retrieved chunks to diagnose why

**Key Metrics:**
- **Retrieval precision:** Are the retrieved chunks actually relevant?
- **Retrieval recall:** Did we find ALL the relevant chunks?
- **Faithfulness:** Does the generated answer actually match what the retrieved chunks say? (Or did the model hallucinate despite the context?)

**Common RAG Failures:**
- **Bad chunking:** Chunks too large → noise. Chunks too small → missing context.
- **Wrong K:** Too few results → missing information. Too many → confusing the model.
- **Embedding mismatch:** User asks a question in a different style than the documents were written → low similarity scores despite relevance.
- **The model ignores the context:** It generates from its training data instead of the retrieved chunks. This is a prompt engineering problem.

Prompts.Description: What does RAG stand for? (capitalize each word)
What RAG metric measures whether the generated answer actually matches the retrieved chunks? (one word, lowercase)
Name the three stages of a RAG pipeline in order. (comma-separated, lowercase)

Concepts.Title: Vector Databases & Indexing
Concepts.Description: Storing and searching embeddings at scale — HNSW, approximate nearest neighbors, and choosing a vector DB.
Concepts.LessonMarkdown:
### Vector Databases & Indexing

A **vector database** is purpose-built for storing and searching embedding vectors. Unlike a traditional database that matches exact values, a vector DB finds the most **similar** vectors using distance metrics.
